title: Pelican to Murder — Level 5 Senior Detective
description: Level 5 version with layered document evidence, delayed clue meaning, and harder final logic.

answerKey.method: Used a duplicate staff badge to move Lena’s emergency kit and frame Owen’s office login
answerKey.motive: To hide that she had been rerouting event deposits through a shell vendor
answerKey.keyEvidence: Duplicate badge access, shell vendor ledger, and metadata showing a staged Owen login
answerKey.explanation: Marisol built the strongest frame by making Owen’s login look responsible, but the badge audit shows a duplicate credential moving between the office and service hall. Her shell vendor ledger explains the motive, and the metadata proves the Owen login was staged after the fact.

publicClues.title: Case Opened
publicClues.text: Pelican to Murder begins as a live investigation. This session is Level 5: Senior Detective Case.

publicClues.title: Difficulty Loaded
publicClues.text: This is Level 5: Senior Detective Case. Evidence and answers are unique to this level.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 1 Available
publicClues.text: Round 1 is ready. Answer the checkpoint after reviewing the duplicate badge.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 2 Available
publicClues.text: Round 2 is ready. Compare timeline movement before answering.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 3 Available
publicClues.text: Round 3 is ready. Digital evidence now matters.

publicClues.title: Checkpoint 4 Available
publicClues.text: Round 4 is ready. Find the motive evidence.

publicClues.title: Final Accusation Open
publicClues.text: Final questions are open. Complete all 10 total questions before the lock.

phone.title: Phone Evidence 1
phone.text: Lena recorded a voicemail saying the fake vendor was not Owen’s only problem.

phone.title: Phone Evidence 2
phone.text: Marisol called the venue landline from the finance desk during the blackout window.

messages.title: Messages Evidence 1
messages.text: Lena messaged Marisol: “I found the second vendor sheet.”

messages.title: Messages Evidence 2
messages.text: Marisol replied: “Do not send that to anyone until I explain.”

maps.title: Maps Evidence 1
maps.text: A staff badge path moves from finance to the office, then to the service hall.

maps.title: Maps Evidence 2
maps.text: Marisol’s phone stayed still, but the duplicate badge kept moving.

bank.title: Bank Evidence 1
bank.text: The sponsor deposits route through a shell vendor named Bayou Night Logistics.

bank.title: Bank Evidence 2
bank.text: Bayou Night Logistics lists Marisol’s apartment mailbox as its billing contact.

photos.title: Photos Evidence 1
photos.text: A photo of the office desk shows Owen’s login open while his phone is visible at the bar.

photos.title: Photos Evidence 2
photos.text: A service hall photo shows Lena’s emergency kit behind a folded linen cart.

social.title: Social Evidence 1
social.text: A guest clip includes a reflected badge lanyard with Marisol’s finance color.

social.title: Social Evidence 2
social.text: The public clip is cropped so the lanyard reflection is gone.

contacts.title: Contacts Evidence 1
contacts.text: Marisol’s contacts include the shell vendor tax preparer.

contacts.title: Contacts Evidence 2
contacts.text: Lena saved the same preparer under “ask about fake logistics.”

notes.title: Notes Evidence 1
notes.text: Detective note: the phone location and badge location do not belong to the same person.

notes.title: Notes Evidence 2
notes.text: Detective note: a framed login is only useful if the real thief controls the documents.

files.title: Files Evidence 1
files.text: Metadata shows Owen’s login was used from a finance desk terminal, not his office computer.

files.title: Files Evidence 2
files.text: The duplicate badge report was exported and deleted from Marisol’s account.

browser.title: Browser Evidence 1
browser.text: Marisol searched how to clone a staff badge audit export.

browser.title: Browser Evidence 2
browser.text: She also searched shell vendor reporting thresholds.

openingNarration: Detectives, welcome to Pelican to Murder. You are playing Level 5: Senior Detective Case. Study each app carefully. Every level has its own truth path, evidence trail, and final answer set.
revealScript: Case closed. This was Level 5: Senior Detective Case. The culprit was Marisol Vega. The method: Used a duplicate staff badge to move Lena’s emergency kit and frame Owen’s office login. The motive: To hide that she had been rerouting event deposits through a shell vendor. The proof: Duplicate badge access, shell vendor ledger, and metadata showing a staged Owen login.

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 1: What detail makes this case harder than a simple office frame?

options.label: The badge path and phone location do not match

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 2: Which clue makes the Owen frame suspicious?

options.label: Owen’s phone is visible at the bar while his login is active

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 3: Which metadata clue matters most?

options.label: Metadata places Owen’s login at the finance desk terminal

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 4: What financial clue reveals the motive?

options.label: Deposits rerouted through Bayou Night Logistics

questions.prompt: Checkpoint 5: Which clue pairing defeats the frame?

options.label: Duplicate badge path plus hidden emergency kit location

questions.prompt: Final 2: How was the murder carried out?

options.label: Marisol used a duplicate badge to move the emergency kit and frame Owen
To hide the shell vendor deposit scheme

questions.prompt: Final 4: Which evidence best proves it?

options.label: Duplicate badge audit, shell ledger, and staged login metadata
Cole said the booth was loud
Marisol said finance never touched the office login
Tessa said she stayed at the rail

briefingSegments.title: Level 5 Briefing: The Perfect Frame
briefingSegments.text: Pelican to Mars was loud enough to hide movement and organized enough to leave records. That combination made the crime both risky and clever. Lena Broussard had been digging into event deposits, staff credentials, and vendor payments that seemed to pass through the wrong hands. By the time she arrived, someone may have already decided that the best way to survive was not just to silence her, but to point the evidence somewhere else. In a Senior Detective case, the obvious suspect may be part of the design. Look for the frame before you trust the picture.

briefingSegments.title: The Crime: Evidence That Points Too Cleanly
briefingSegments.text: After Lena died, the first evidence trail seemed almost too convenient. A login pointed toward one office. Access records suggested one path. Financial pressure seemed to belong to one person. But clean evidence can be planted, copied, or staged after the fact. This level asks you to question whether the record is true or merely useful. A duplicate credential, a shell vendor trail, and metadata around a staged login may matter more than the loudest accusation. The method was not only physical. It was administrative, digital, and designed to make detectives stop looking too soon.

briefingSegments.title: The Victim: Lena Broussard
briefingSegments.text: Lena understood the quiet side of corruption: deposits moved in small amounts, vendor names that sounded legitimate, access logs that looked boring until placed beside the right timestamp. She was close to showing that money from the event was being rerouted through a false vendor structure. That made her dangerous to someone who knew how to manipulate records. Lena’s death was not just about panic. It was about preventing a paper trail from becoming a public trail. Whoever acted needed both access to physical spaces and enough digital knowledge to redirect suspicion.

briefingSegments.title: The Circle: Motives Inside Motives
briefingSegments.text: Marisol Vega knew staff movement, credentials, and back-of-house routines, but Senior Detective cases do not reward assumptions. Owen Landry looks financially exposed. Cole Vinet controls timing. Brielle Hart controls public narrative. Tessa Marchand understands service flow. Every suspect can be made to look wrong if you stop at the first clue. Relationships to Lena include professional pressure, financial fear, reputation damage, and resentment over who controlled the event. The real motive will connect to the evidence that remains after you remove planted direction, coincidence, and convenient blame.

briefingSegments.title: Your Assignment: Prove the Frame
briefingSegments.text: This is a Senior Detective case. Treat every clean answer as suspicious until it is supported by independent evidence. Compare badge access, metadata, ledgers, messages, and physical movement. Ask whether a suspect actually did something or whether someone wanted their name attached to the record. Checkpoints will force you to commit before later clues make the pattern easier. The final accusation will require the culprit, method, motive, key evidence, and the false trail. When the investigation begins, do not chase the person the evidence points to first. Chase the person who shaped the evidence.
